segments.s766-g1: فرخ‌زاد هرمزد is a compound proper name: فرخ‌زاد (personal name) followed by هرمزد as an epithet/second name. با + آب چشم is an instrumental/associated phrase: با (with) + آب (literally “water”) + چشم (eye) = “with the water of the eye,” i.e. with tears. The noun phrase follows the name, as is common in Persian.
segments.s766-g2: به اروندرود اندر آمد is a motion clause: به ... اندر آمد = “entered into …” (به = toward/into; اندر آمد = came/entered). اروندرود is the destination. بخشم here is problematic: it appears as a short archaic form meaning something like “in anger / wrath” (ب + خشم), rather than the listed verbal senses; it functions adverbially modifying آمد. Because the supplied lemma senses do not match this usage, its sense is marked -1.
segments.s766-g3: به کرخ اندر آمد is again a locative-motion phrase: “came into Karkh.” یکی حمله برد is an indefinite subject + verb phrase: یکی (a certain/one) + حمله برد (an onslaught was made / launched). Here برد is part of the verbal idiom حمله بردن = “to make/launch an attack,” so the whole means “an attack came / was launched.”
segments.s766-g4: که introduces a result clause (’such that’). از نیزه‌داران = “from the spearmen,” نماند is the past negative: ‘was not left / remained,’ ایچ = “anything/nothing” (negated), and گرد here functions as an intensifying/colloquial particle roughly ‘at all’ or ‘around.’ The clause therefore says no spearman remained.
segments.s766-g5: هم آنگه is a temporal/discourse phrase: هم (also/too) + آنگه (then) = “then, likewise.” ز بغداد بیرون شدند = “they went out from Baghdad” (ز = from, بیرون = out, شدند = past plural auxiliary/verb ‘they departed’). The verb شد(ند) functions as the past-form auxiliary meaning ‘went/left’ here.
segments.s766-g6: سوی رزم جستن is a purpose phrase: سوی (toward) + رزم (battle) + جستن (infinitive ‘to seek’) = ‘toward seeking battle’ (i.e. to look for combat). به هامون شدند completes the motion: به (to) هامون (the Hamun/plain) + شدند (they went/arrived).
segments.s766-g7: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning ‘when.’ برخاست (rose/arose) is the past verb, and گرد نبرد = ‘the dust (or din) of battle’ (گرد used as the noun ‘dust’); از میان = ‘from the middle / from the midst.’ The phrase literally: ‘When the battle-dust rose from the midst…’
segments.s766-g8: شکست is the subject ‘defeat,’ اندر آمد is the light-verb phrase ‘came upon / befell,’ and به ایرانیان marks the affected party: ‘to the Iranians.’ Together: ‘defeat came upon the Iranians’ i.e. they were defeated. Note آمد is used here in the sense ‘happen / befall.’
segments.s766-g137: شما (you, plural/formal) + ساز گیرید is an addressed imperative: گیرید is the 2nd-person plural/formal form (imperative/optative) of گرفتن ‘take’; here it means ‘take up/play (an instrument)’. با پای او is an adverbial phrase literally ‘with/at his foot(s)’ — a poetic idiom meaning ‘at his feet’ or ‘before him/by his command’, modifying the preceding imperative.
segments.s766-g138: گذر نیست is a verbless existential clause: گذر ‘passage/movement’ + نیست ‘is not’ → ‘there is no passage/allowance’. با گردش و رای او is a circumstantial phrase ‘by/through his گردش (turn/change/rotation) and رای (plan/counsel/decision)’, i.e. nothing happens except according to his change and his counsel; گردش here = change/turn (of events) and رای = decision/counsel.
segments.s766-g139: وزان پس is a temporal connective: وزان (from that/thereafter) + پس (then/after) = ‘after that/thereupon’. به بازارگانان چین is a dative phrase ‘to the merchants of China’ (به + direct object), indicating the addressees of the speech.
segments.s766-g140: چنین گفت is ‘thus he said’ (چنین = ‘thus/so’, گفت here functions as the past tense ‘said’ introducing reported speech). کاکنون (کـا + اکنون) is an emphatic temporal adverb ‘now, even now’ marking the opening of the reported clause; به ایران‌زمین is the locative ‘in/to the land of Iran’. The whole segment frames the reported utterance.
segments.s766-g141: مباشید is the negative imperative/optative (‘do not be’ / ‘do not (you)’) in 2nd-person plural. یک چند is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘for a short while / even for a little time’. کز (contraction of که + ز) here means ‘from’ or ‘out of’ and introduces the source/target: تازیان = ‘the Arabs’. Together: ‘Do not, even briefly, [seek anything] from the Arabs.’
segments.s766-g142: بدین is the fixed compound meaning ‘by/with this’ (بـ + دین = ‘by this’). سود جستن is a noun+infinitive construction: سود ‘profit/benefit’ + جستن ‘to seek’ → ‘to seek profit’. سرآید is a 3rd-person singular present/future-predictive verb ‘will come/ensue’ (literary use), and زیان is the result noun ‘harm/loss’. The clause states the consequence: ‘From seeking such profit, harm will come.’
segments.s766-g143: ازو بازگشتند is a past-tense clause: ازو ‘from him/it’ + بازگشتند ‘they returned’ (3rd-person plural past). با درد و جوش is a coordinated adverbial of manner: با + درد ‘with pain/sorrow’ and جوش ‘ardor/upheaval’ (here emotional agitation), describing how they returned.
segments.s766-g144: ز تیمار is another causal/temporal prepositional phrase: ز (poetic equivalent of از) ‘out of/from’ + تیمار ‘care/anxiety’ → ‘from anxiety/worry’. با ناله و با خروش repeats با to link two concomitant manners: ناله ‘moan/lament’ and خروش ‘cry/uproar’ — together specifying the sounds they made as they left.
segments.s766-g49: چنان هم ("just so/so too") links this line to the previous statement; که introduces the following noun phrase. کهتر is the noun 'the lesser/one of lower rank' serving as the subject of the subordinate clause, and به فرمان شاه is a prepositional phrase ('by the king's command') modifying that subject. Word order is typical Persian: adverbial linker → subordinating particle → subject phrase → modifier.
segments.s766-g50: بد و نیک ('bad and good') is a coordinated object/theme. باید is the modal 'must/ought' introducing a subordinate content clause (note the following که). دارد is present indicative of داشتن used here with نگاه ('to have/keep a gaze'), so دارد نگاه = 'keeps/has a watch.' The clause therefore says that the lesser must keep watch over bad and good.
segments.s766-g51: جهاندار (literally 'world-ruler') is the subject 'the sovereign.' باید is the necessity modal 'must' and که introduces the required complement clause. او را is the accusative definite pronoun 'him' (را marks the direct object) and به رنج is a prepositional phrase 'into/ in suffering' that links to the verb in the next segment. This segment sets up the object and circumstance that the following verb will negate.
segments.s766-g52: نماند is the negative present/past-stem meaning 'be left/not remain' (here 'not be left'). بجای in this context means 'in that place/condition' (i.e. left in hardship). The coordinating و links the clause to شود ('become/happen'), which here means 'be (made to) go' or 'goes' toward the following goal. سوی گنج is 'toward the treasure/treasury.' The sequence expresses: 'he should not be left in hardship, but (instead) go toward the treasury.'
segments.s766-g53: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones/nobles.' خواندند is past plural 'they called/sang/cried out.' آفرین is the interjection they uttered ('Bravo!'/'Well done!'). The intervening برو is a poetic/idiomatic intensifier here (appearing with خواندند to mean 'called out loudly'); it does not carry its literal 'go' sense in this construction.
segments.s766-g54: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that.' اینست is the fused form of این است ('this is'). آیین is the predicate 'custom/practice/ritual' and شاهان is the genitive 'of kings.' دین here should be read as 'duty/obligation' or 'established practice' — together آیین شاهان دین expresses 'the customary duty/practice of kings.'
segments.s766-g55: نگه کن is an imperative 'keep watch/observe' (نگه + the auxiliary imperative کن). کنون is the adverb 'now.' تا here functions as a subordinating particle introducing an indirect interrogative/purpose clause 'so that/that (we may know) what ...' چه فرمان دهی is the indirect question 'what order you give' — دهی is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive of دادن 'to give.'
segments.s766-g56: چه خواهی is 'what you want/will' (خواهی = 2nd‑person present 'you want/will'). و links the items in the list. با ما is the prepositional phrase 'with us.' چه پیمان نهی is 'what covenant you will make': پیمان 'covenant/pledge' is the object, and نهی here is a verbal form (2nd‑person subjunctive from نهادن/نَهَدَن) meaning 'you shall set/make' — it is not the nominal 'prohibition' sense listed in the lemma entry, so treat it as a verb meaning 'make/establish' a pact.
segments.s766-g121: که is the subordinating/complementizer 'that' introducing the clause. The phrase has SOV order: ما (we) is the subject, بوم آباد (būm ābād) is a noun + adjective compound 'prosperous homeland' (noun before adjective, common in classical style), and بگذاشتیم is the past-tense verb 'we made/established' (ب- prefix + گذاشتیم). The clause thus reads literally 'that we the prosperous homeland made.'
segments.s766-g122: This clause is also SOV: جهان ('the world') is the theme, در پناه تو is a prepositional phrase 'in/under your protection' (در + پناه + تو), and پنداشتیم is the past-tense verb 'we supposed/thought'. The verb comes at the end and takes the preceding phrase as its content (we supposed that the world was under your protection).
segments.s766-g123: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now' placed at the front. داغ‌دل (lit. 'burnt/aching heart') is an adjectival or adverbial phrase indicating sorrow ('heart-sore'). نزد خاقان is the locative/prepositional phrase 'to/into the presence of the khagan' (نزد = 'in the presence of'), and شویم (from شدن) is a present-subjunctive/optative form 'let us go / let us become / let us present ourselves' used here to urge movement into the khagan's presence. Word order places the temporal/adverbial elements first and the verb at the end.
segments.s766-g124: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (or 'because of') introducing the source/agent (تازی = 'the Arabs'). سوی مرز دهقان is a directional phrase: سوی = 'toward', مرز = 'border', دهقان here refers to the dehqān (landed gentry / Persian local magnates). شویم repeats the optative/imperative 'let us go' at the end, creating a parallel pair of clauses. So the segment reads 'from the Arabs, toward the dehqān border let us go.'
segments.s766-g125: شهنشاه is the subject 'the shahanshah, emperor'. مژگان 'eyelashes' is the affected part, and the adjectival phrase مژگان پر از آب uses پر + از + noun to mean 'full of (water)' — here 'filled with water' i.e. tears. کرد is the past-tense verb 'made/caused' (here rendering 'his eyelashes became filled' or 'he caused his eyelashes to fill'), placed at the end as typical SOV order.
segments.s766-g126: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus'), with گفت the past-tense verb 'said' (note: the provided senses list does not include a plain 'said' sense, so index -1 is used for that mismatch). با نامداران is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the renowned ones' (با + نامداران), and بدرد here functions adverbially indicating sorrow/harm ('to cause pain / in pain'), qualifying the manner or result of his speech. The verb گفت governs the whole phrase.
segments.s766-g127: که introduces a subordinate exhortation 'that'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/constantly' or 'all together'. به یزدان is the dative/prepositional phrase 'to God', نیایش is the noun 'prayer/supplication', and کنید is the present-subjunctive/imperative plural 'you do' (here used as a polite command: 'pray'). The verb comes at the end as is normal.
segments.s766-g128: ستایش 'praise' is the object; ورا is the clitic object 'to him' (referring to God), در فزایش is a locution 'in increase' meaning 'so that it increases' or 'in increasing', and کنید repeats the imperative/subjunctive 'do' (i.e. 'increase'). The order places the object phrase before the verb, as is typical in Persian.
segments.s766-g153: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گرگان is the place name 'Gorgan'. بیامد is the past-tense verb 'he came' (perfective). سوی follows بیامد and marks direction 'toward/against' (here 'approached'); راه (object) precedes the verb بست, and بست is the past of بستن 'to close/block'. Put together the clause means 'He came from Gorgan, moved toward and blocked the road' with the subject omitted because it is the same actor throughout the line.
segments.s766-g154: پرآژنگ is a prefixed adjective (پر- 'full of' + آژنگ) meaning 'adorned/ornamented' and here modifies رخسار (countenance/face). The conjunction و links this noun phrase to the next. دل is 'heart' and نادرست is a postpositive adjective 'wrong/crooked' (moral or inwardly untrustworthy). Together: 'an ornamented countenance and a crooked/false heart.'
segments.s766-g113: "همه" precedes a coordinated list and means "all/every/the whole" here; "بوم آباد" is a two-word phrase meaning "prosperous/inhabited homeland" (noun + adjective), then the nouns "فرزند" and "گنج" continue the list (each singular standing for the collective). The order is typical Persian: determinative "همه" + nouns, listing what will remain.
segments.s766-g114: "بمانیم" is first-person plural present/subjunctive (optative/jussive) of ماندن — here "may we remain / we shall remain / we will keep" and is the main verbal action applying to the preceding list. The coordinating "و" links this clause to the next action; "با تو" is a prepositional phrase "with you" modifying the following verb; "گزینیم" is also first-person plural subjunctive of گزیدن meaning "we choose/prefer"; "رنج" is the direct object "suffering/hardship."
segments.s766-g115: "زمانه" is a noun meaning "age/time/the world"; "نخواهیم" is the negative present of خواستن in first-person plural and here expresses "we do not want/wish" (in classical usage it can carry an optative flavor); "بی تخت تو" is a prepositional phrase: "بی" = without, "تخت" = throne, "تو" = your — so "we do not want an age without your throne."
segments.s766-g116: "مبادا" is an optative/prohibitive particle meaning "may it not be / God forbid." "که" introduces the subordinate clause. "پیچان" is an adjective here meaning "twisting/crooked (changeable)," and "شود" is the third-person singular subjunctive/present of شدن "to become" — together "پیچان شود" = "become twisted/turned." "بخت تو" is the subject/possessed noun phrase "your fortune/fate."
segments.s766-g117: "همه" = everyone/all; "با تو" = with you; "آییم" is first-person plural subjunctive/jussive of آمدن (آییدن) often functioning as an exhortation "let us come/go" or future "we will come." "تا" here introduces purpose/result ("so that / in order that"), and "روزگار" means "fate/time" in the sense of what happens (here translated "fate").
segments.s766-g118: "چه" is an interrogative/exclamatory pronoun "what"; "بازی" here means "a play/trick" (i.e., what turn or trick events will play); "کند" is third-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن "to do/make" ("will do/play"); "در دم" = "in the moment" (literally "in the breath/moment"); and "کارزار" means "battle/battlefield." Together: "what game (fate) will play at the moment of battle."
segments.s766-g119: "ز" is the poetic preposition "of/from." "خاقانیان" is a plural proper/collective name — here referring to the people called Khaghanians (men of the Khaghan / imperial people). "آنک" is an archaic relative/ demonstrative meaning "those who / that which." "بد" functions adverbially here as "ill/evil/illly," and "چرب‌گوی" is a compound adjective/noun "smooth‑tongued / flattering speaker." Together it describes "those of the Khaghanians who were ill‑smooth‑tongued (flatterers)."
segments.s766-g120: "به" = toward/to; "خاک سیه" = "black earth/soil" (noun + adjective; adjective follows noun in Persian poetry); "برنهادند" is an archaic past-tense verb (3rd pl.) from برنهادن meaning "to raise/put/lay upon" — used idiomatically here as "laid/laid down/bowed" — and "روی" = "face." The clause means they laid/bowed their faces to the black earth (i.e., prostrated or cast down their faces).
segments.s766-g17: This noun phrase names the parties being contrasted: توی is an archaic/poetic form addressing 'you' (here emphatic/subjective), یک تن means 'one person' (تن used to mean a single person), و is the coordinating conjunction 'and/while' linking the two halves, and دشمنان صد هزار presents the opponents as 'enemies, a hundred thousand'. Word order places the small-numbered subject first, then the large-numbered opponents for contrast.
segments.s766-g18: میان جهان functions as a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in/among the world' (میان = 'in the middle of/among', جهان = 'world'), چون introduces an interrogative/exclamatory sense 'how' or 'in what way' here, and کنی (present/subjunctive-like form from کن/کردن) + کارزار = 'you make/perform battle' or 'you wage war'. The verb form کنی is the classical 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive used in questions or admonitions.
segments.s766-g19: برو is the imperative 'go'; تا here means 'as far as / to' (marking the goal/direction), سوی is 'toward', and بیشه نارون is the compound noun 'elm grove' (بیشه = grove, نارون = elm). Together they form a directional command: 'Go to/toward the elm-grove.'
segments.s766-g20: This clause states the intended/resultant condition. جهانی (adjectival/noun) refers to 'the world' or 'worldly/public status'; شود is the 3rd‑person singular present/potential 'become / may be'; بر تو = 'upon you' (بر = 'on/upon' + تو = 'you'); بر انجمن = 'before/at the assembly' (بر used in a locative/relational sense toward/at + انجمن 'assembly'). The sequence gives a purposive/result clause: 'so that the world may be upon you at the assembly.'
segments.s766-g21: وزان is a fused prepositional phrase 'and/from that' connecting back to the previous location; جایگاه means 'place' (or position); چون before a proper name here means 'like/as' (simile); فریدون is the proper name invoked for comparison; برو is the imperative 'go'. The phrase reads 'And from that place, as Fereydun, go.'
segments.s766-g22: جوانی یکی is a noun phrase 'a young man' or 'one youth' (جوانی = youth / young person, یکی = one / a certain); کار = 'deed/enterprise'; برساز is an imperative/optative form 'bring about/perform/make' (برساز = 'set up/achieve'); نو = 'new'. The sequence issues a directive or wish: 'let a single young man accomplish a new deed.'
segments.s766-g23: This is a simple past narrative clause: فرخ‌زاد گفت 'Farrokhzad said/ spoke' (گفت is past tense), و = 'and', جهانبان شنید 'Jahanban heard' (شنید past of شنیدن). The two actions are coordinated sequencing speech and its being heard.
segments.s766-g24: یکی دیگر اندیشه آمد پدید is a concise idiom: یکی دیگر = 'one other / another', اندیشه = 'thought/idea', آمد پدید = 'came forth/appeared' (آمد past 'came' + پدید 'manifest/appear'). Together: 'another thought came into being/appeared.'
segments.s766-g97: ببود is an archaic past-tense copula meaning 'it was' used impersonally. آن شب و بامداد پگاه is a time expression ('that night and the break of dawn') following the copula; note the poetic doubling (بامداد / پگاه) for emphasis on the early-morning moment.
segments.s766-g98: گرانمایگان is the plural subject 'the illustrious/noble ones.' برگرفتند is a past plural verb 'they took up / set out' and راه is the object 'the road' — together برگرفتند راه forms the idiom 'to take the road / set out.' Word order here (subject - verb - object) is a poetic variation but clear in context.
segments.s766-g99: ز بغداد is a prepositional phrase 'from Baghdad.' راه خراسان is 'the road of/to Khorasan' with خراسان as a postmodifier of راه. گرفت is the simple past 'took' (here used with راه to mean 'chose/took the road').
segments.s766-g100: همه رنجها 'all the hardships' is the object phrase. بر دل 'upon the heart' marks where the feeling is borne; آسان گرفت is a compound predicate: آسان گرفتن (here in past گرفت) means 'to take lightly / to bear lightly.' The implicit subject is the same as previous agents (the nobles).
segments.s766-g101: بزرگان ایران is a noun phrase 'the great men of Iran.' همه is a distributive 'all' modifying them. پر ز درد is an adjectival phrase 'full of sorrow' (پر 'full (of)' + ز 'of' + درد 'sorrow'), placed after the noun as is typical in Persian poetical syntax.
segments.s766-g102: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed / went away.' با شاه آزادمرد is an accompanying prepositional phrase 'with the noble king,' where آزادمرد is an epithet functioning after شاه.
segments.s766-g103: برو بر here functions as an exclamatory/imperative phrase ('go on' / 'onward') that they utter; همی‌خواندند is a classical continuous past form ('they were singing/kept singing') — the prefix همی marks ongoing or habitual action — and آفرین is the interjection 'Bravo!' that they repeatedly cried.
segments.s766-g104: که introduces a subordinate clause expressing a wish/ban. بی تو 'without you' is the condition; مبادا is a classical optative/negative 'may it not be / God forbid' and here negates existence: 'مبادا زمان و زمین' = 'may time and earth not exist' (i.e., 'may there be no time or earth without you'). The verb 'to exist' is omitted and understood.
segments.s766-g33: به آمل is a prepositional locative phrase ('to/into Amol' — here 'in/at Amol'). پرستندگان is the nominal subject 'the worshippers'. تواند is the present/optative third-person form of توانستن (here a modal: 'are able/can'); classical Persian often places the verb after its subject as seen here.
segments.s766-g34: به ساری is another locative phrase ('in/at Sari'); همه بندگان is the subject 'all the people/subjects'; تواند is the same modal verb 'are able/can'. The clause is parallel to the previous one and uses the same subject–verb order.
segments.s766-g35: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. لشکر فراوان is the noun phrase 'the army (made) abundant/large'. شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن 'becomes'. بازگرد is the predicate verb 'returns/turns back' presented without an overt person ending (a poetic/older form), so the clause reads 'when the army becomes numerous, it (will) return.'
segments.s766-g36: به مردم marks the indirect/affected party 'to the people'. توان here is the modal root 'can' (present); ساخت is the verbal base 'to make/bring' (used as infinitive/predicate with the modal). ننگ و نبرد are the coordinated objects 'shame and battle' — i.e. 'to cause shame and battle to the people.' Note the auxiliary/modal construction and the poetic dropping/variation of endings.
segments.s766-g37: شما را is the experiencer marked by را ('to/for you'); پسند is a noun 'liking/approval' used predicatively; آید (literally 'comes') forms the impersonal/predicative phrase پسند آید = 'is pleasing'. این گفت‌وگوی is the topicalized noun phrase 'this conversation' that is the thing which may please you.
segments.s766-g38: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice / aloud' (به + manner). گفتند is the past plural 'they said' (reported speech marker). کاین introduces the content clause 'that this ...'; نیست is the negative copula 'is not'; روی here is a noun 'face/appearance/reputability' used idiomatically: 'نیست روی' = 'is not seemly/appropriate.'
segments.s766-g39: شهنشاه گفت is a simple subject–verb clause 'the king said'. این سخن is the direct object 'this speech/remark'. درخورست is an adjectival predicate formed with درخور + -ست (copula) meaning 'is fitting/appropriate.' The copula appears fused as -ست as in classical usage.
segments.s766-g40: مرا (here split in the word-list as م + را) functions as 'to me/for me' (experiencer). در دل is the locative phrase 'in (the) heart' and اندیشه دیگرست is the predicate 'a different/other thought exists' (دیگرست = 'is other/different'). The whole clause means 'I have another thought in my heart' or 'there is another thought in my heart (for me)'.
segments.s766-g57: Literal order is thematic object + modifier + verbal phrase + subject: مهان را ('to the guests', marked by را as the recipient) + چنین ('thus') + پاسخ آورد ('gave an answer' — the compound verb پاسخ آوردن) with شاه placed after the verb as the subject. In classical/poetic Persian this postposed subject is normal; 'پاسخ آورد' functions as 'replied, gave a reply.'
segments.s766-g58: کز here introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('because / from'), so کز اندیشه = 'from thinking' or 'because of (my) thought'. گردد is the present/subjunctive form of گردیدن/گشتن meaning 'become' (here 'becomes ruined'); دل من is the subject + possessor 'my heart', and تباه is the predicate adjective 'ruined/corrupt.' Word order is poetic (verb before subject) but grammatically: 'because of thought my heart becomes ruined.'
segments.s766-g59: همانا is an emphatic particle ('verily / behold') introducing the statement. که here introduces the content clause ('that/so that'); سوی خراسان شویم = 'we go/toward Khorasan' with سوی marking direction and شویم (1st-person-plural form of شدن/شدن) used optatively/hortatively — 'let us go' or 'we shall go' in epic style. In classical Persian شویم often expresses a wished-for or future action.
segments.s766-g60: ز = 'from / because of' introduces the cause: ز پیکار دشمن = 'from / because of the enemy's battle.' تن‌آسان is a predicate adjective meaning 'easy-bodied / at ease / living in comfort.' شویم repeats the same verbal form 'we shall be / we shall become,' so the clause means 'we shall be at ease (freed) from the enemy's fighting.' The structure is cause (ز ...) + result (تن‌آسان شویم).
segments.s766-g61: کزان سو = 'from that side/direction' (کزان = 'from which/of that'), فراوان modifies the following predicate as 'plentifully / a great ...'. مرا here marks the beneficiary/indirect object ('for me' / 'to me'), and لشکرست is the copular construction (لشکر است) meaning 'there is a host / an army.' Poetic spacing separates elements, but the sense is 'From that direction there is for me a plentiful army.'
segments.s766-g62: همه پهلوانان = 'all the champions.' کنداورست is a single poetic/predicative word attached here to state their condition (an archaic/compound adjective/verb form meaning they are arrayed/engaged/assembled); in context it functions as the predicate about the champions. The exact lexical elements are archaic/poetic, so the phrase reads 'all the champions are so (ready/assembled/active).'
segments.s766-g63: A list of dignitaries: بزرگان ('the great ones / elders / magnates') و ترکان ('and the Turks') followed by a title خاقان چین where the genitive is implied: 'the khagan of China' (خاقان + چین 'China'). Word order is noun conjunction + qualifying title; چین names the realm of that khagan.
segments.s766-g64: بیایند is the 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative ('may they come' / 'they will come'); و connects clauses; بر ما is a prepositional phrase 'upon us / to us' marking the target of the action; کنند آفرین is a light-verb construction where کنند ('they make/do') takes آفرین ('acclamation/praise') as its object: 'they will come and give (us) praise/acclaim.' In sum: 'they will come and acclaim us.'
segments.s766-g89: که هر چند here introduces a concessive clause ('though/however much'). هر چند is a fixed idiom meaning 'however much' (هر + چند). بر + گوهر is a prepositional phrase 'upon the gem', and افسون کنی uses the deverbal noun افسون with the auxiliary/verb کنی (present/subjunctive 2sg of کردن) to mean 'you enchant' or 'you cast a spell upon'.
segments.s766-g90: بکوشی is the present/subjunctive 2sg of کوشیدن ('you try' / 'you may try'), continuing the concessive sense. کزو = از + او (contracted) 'from it / from which', referring back to the gem. رنگ بیرون کنی combines the noun رنگ with the adverb بیرون and the verb کنی ('you make/bring out') meaning 'you (try to) bring the color out'.
segments.s766-g91: چو here functions as 'when' (temporal: 'when/as'), introducing a clause describing the act of creation. پروردگارش is 'its Lord/creator' (پروردگار plus possessive -ش). چنان 'thus/so' modifies آفرید, which is the past tense 'created'.
segments.s766-g92: The main clause: تو (you) is subject; بر بند یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'on/to the bond/fetter of God' (بر + بند + یزدان) describing what follows; نیابی is the negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'you do not find' or 'you will not find' (ن- + یابی); کلید (key) is the object. Word order is slightly inverted but reads as 'you will not find the key to God's bond.'
segments.s766-g93: ازیشان is the fused prepositional phrase از + ایشان 'from them' or 'of/from these (those previously mentioned)'; نبرند is a jussive/optative negative form meaning 'may they not take' (ن- + برَند); رنگ و نژاد are the direct objects 'color and lineage' that would be taken. The clause expresses a wish that these not be taken away.
segments.s766-g94: تو را is the topical/object-marked 'for you' (تو + را). جز means 'except', introducing the restriction 'except greatness and kingship'. بزرگی و شاهی are the nouns 'greatness and kingship', and مباد is an optative particle meaning 'may it not be' or 'may there be none other', so the whole phrase is a wish: 'may you have nothing but greatness and kingship.'
segments.s766-g95: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت is the past stem of گفتن 'said' used here; word order 'to him said' is typical of older Persian). شاه‌ای + هژبر ژیان is a vocative: شاه‌ای 'O king' followed by the name/epithet هژبر ژیان (Hezbar Zhīān) addressing the addressee.
segments.s766-g96: ازین is از + این 'from this / of this' (here 'this' = this ordeal). آزمایش is 'test/ordeal' (آزمایش), ندارد is present negative 'has not / does not have' (ندارد = 'there is no / it does not cause'), and زیان is 'harm/loss'. Together: 'this trial has no harm' → 'this trial will do you no harm.'
segments.s766-g145: فرخ‌زاد هرمزد is a compound proper name functioning as the grammatical subject; لشکر is the direct object (the army). براند is a past third‑person singular verb (poetic/older form with the b‑prefix) meaning 'drove' or 'led'. Word order is somewhat inverted for epic diction: subject + object + verb.
segments.s766-g146: ز ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' (ز = poetic 'from'); جهاندیدگان is the object 'the worldly‑wise/experienced ones' and is followed by the definite object marker را. بخواند here is a past third‑person singular verb (poetic form) meaning 'called' or 'summoned' rather than 'read'.
segments.s766-g147: همی‌رفت is a progressive/past imperfective ('was going') common in epic Persian. با ناله و درد is a manner phrase 'with moaning and sorrow' modifying the verb. شاه is the subject; in this poetic line the subject follows the manner phrase (inversion for rhythm).
segments.s766-g148: سپهبد is a title ('spahbed', commander) appositional to شاه. به پیش اندرون is a compound directional phrase 'toward the presence/inner court' (به + پیش + اندرون). با سپاه 'with the army' is an accompanying adverbial. Word order places these adverbials after the subject for poetic flow.
segments.s766-g149: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. منزل به منزل is a repetitive adverbial 'from stage to stage' (i.e. from one lodging/stage to the next). بیامد is past 'came'. بری here is an adjectival/state word 'free, at ease' describing his condition when arriving (poetic placement after the verb).
segments.s766-g150: برآسود is past 'was at rest' or 'found repose'. یک چند is an adverbial 'for a while / for a few moments'. با رود و می means 'with رود and wine' where رود is an archaic musical instrument (here 'lute'/'string instrument') and می is 'wine' — so 'with music and wine'.
segments.s766-g151: ز ری سوی گرگان is a movement clause 'from Rey toward Gorgan' (ز = poetic 'from', سوی = 'toward'). بیامد is past 'came'. چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = 'as/like', باد = 'wind'), linking his manner of movement to the next comment.
segments.s766-g152: همی‌بود is the progressive past 'was being' or 'kept being'. یک چند 'for a while'. ناشاد و شاد are contrasting adjectives 'unhappy and happy' — a poetic way of saying he was alternately sad and glad; the coordination shows fluctuating mood.
segments.s766-g9: 'فرخ‌زاد برگشت و شد نزد شاه' literally orders the constituent parts: proper name + past verb + coordinating 'and' + an auxiliary-like verb + preposition + noun. 'برگشت' is a simple past 'returned'; 'و' links two actions; 'شد' here functions as a past auxiliary/verb meaning 'came' or 'came to be' (a common classical usage: 'شد نزدِ' = 'came to the presence of'); 'نزد شاه' = 'in the presence of the king' (نزد = 'before/in the presence of', شاه = 'king').
segments.s766-g10: 'پر از گرد با آلت رزمگاه' is a nominal phrase describing Farrukhzād's condition/equipment: 'پر از' = 'full of / covered with', here taking 'گرد' as the thing he's full/covered with (dust). 'با' = 'with' introduces accompaniment (what he has with him), and 'آلت رزمگاه' = 'the implements/tools of the battlefield' (آلت = instruments/tools, رزمگاه = battlefield/place of fighting). Word order is typical Persian: adjective/phrase ('پر از گرد') followed by a 'with'-phrase describing what he bears.
segments.s766-g11: 'فرود آمد' is a past compound meaning 'he alighted / came down'. 'از' + 'باره' here indicates origin/cause ('from the burden / from the weight/occasion'), i.e. he descended from his mount or out of the action. 'بردش' in this line appears as a verb form attached with -ش (object clitic), but the senses supplied did not include the 'to perform / to take (prayer)' meaning; the intended sense is 'he performed/observed (نماز)', and 'نماز' is the direct object (ritual prayer). The clause therefore reads 'he alighted and performed the prayer.'
segments.s766-g12: 'دو دیده پر از خون و دل پرگداز' describes his condition: 'دو دیده' = 'two eyes' (subject/possessive topic), 'پر از خون' = 'full of blood' (پر + از + noun = 'full of ...'), 'و' = 'and', 'دل پرگداز' = 'a heart full of burning / searing grief' (پرگداز is a compound adjective meaning 'burning / searing'). Word order places the nouns before their adjectival complements, as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s766-g13: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' where 'بدو' is the dative 'to him' (not the imperative). 'چندین' here functions interrogatively ('for how long' / 'how many [times]') or as 'many/a number of', used to question duration/frequency. 'چه مولی' is a vocative phrase 'O what a master' / 'O master' (چه intensifies or questions the quality), and 'همی' is the classical verbal particle indicating continued/ongoing action (here giving the sense 'do you still / do you continually'). The whole fragment is a direct address: 'he said to him: how long, O master, will you...'.
segments.s766-g14: 'که' introduces the subordinate clause explaining the complaint ('that ...'). 'گاه' here means 'occasionally / at times'. 'کیی' refers to a member of the Kayanian line (the Kayanian), 'را' is the definite-object marker, and 'بشولی' is the imperfective/present form of 'شستن' (to wash) with the prefix 'ب-' and suffix vowel; 'همی' again indicates continued or habitual aspect. Syntactically this yields: 'that at times you wash the Kayanian' — the exact nuance is rhetorical/accusatory (why do you sometimes spare or cleanse the Kayanian?), so the clause is subordinate to the vocative question.
segments.s766-g15: 'ز تخم کیان' = 'from the seed / stock of the Kians' (ز used poetically for 'from'); 'کس جز از تو نماند' = 'no one remained except you' — the negative 'نماند' 'remained' is applied to 'کس' (no one), modified by 'جز از تو' ('except from you / apart from you'). Word order places the exception phrase before the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s766-g16: 'که' introduces a relative/subordinate clause describing 'تو' (you). 'با تاج بر تخت' = 'with the crown upon the throne' (با = with, تاج = crown, بر تخت = on the throne). 'شاید' is the modal adverb 'perhaps / it is possible', and 'نشاند' is an archaic/transitive past form meaning 'placed / seated (someone)' — here it expresses the possibility that he (or someone) might seat you on the throne wearing the crown. The sequence 'شاید نشاند' gives potentialive meaning ('might seat').
segments.s766-g81: The clause begins with the particle که (here = 'that') introducing the injunction. پرهیز is a noun used as the content of the imperative and together with کن (imperative 'do') forms the command 'پرهیز کن' = 'avoid/abstain'. از آن 'from that' marks what to avoid. The second که introduces the subordinate relative clause 'که بد کرده‌ای' = 'which you have done badly' (کرده‌ای is perfect/present-perfect: 'you have done').
segments.s766-g82: This relative clause continues the thought: که (that) + او را (him, object marked by را) + به بیهوده (an adverbial phrase 'in vain/needlessly', with به used adverbially) + آزرده‌ای (perfect 'you have distressed/hurt'). The word order is subject-object-verb in the subordinate clause, and آزرده‌ای is a perfect-form participle used predicatively.
segments.s766-g83: بدان دار اومید is an idiomatic command: بدان (a fused form meaning roughly 'to/that') + دار (imperative or verbal root 'hold/keep') + اومید (امید, 'hope') = 'hold that hope' or 'keep in mind that hope'. کو را here is a relative/archaic 'who/whom' (کو = 'who') marked by را, and به مهر is a prepositional phrase 'by/with love' (به + مهر). The clause attaches to what follows describing whose hope it is.
segments.s766-g84: سر از نیستی بردی اندر سپهر is a poetic transitive clause: سر 'head' (metonymically 'life' or 'being') + از نیستی 'from nonexistence' + بردی (past tense 'you took/carried/raised') + اندر سپهر 'into the sky/heavens'. Word order is object ('سر') + from-phrase + verb + locative phrase; بردی is simple past (you carried/raised).
segments.s766-g85: فرخ‌زاد is the subject (proper name). برهم بزد is a verbal phrase: برهم (together/one-upon-another) + بزد (simple past 'he struck' / 'he struck/clapped') so 'struck together' = 'clapped'. هر دودست is an adverbial expression 'with both hands' (هر = each/every, دودست = two‑handed). The overall order is Subject + verb + manner/strumental phrase.
segments.s766-g86: بدو گفت is the light reporting construction 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' in past; here گفت functions as the verb 'said'). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee; شاه is 'king' and یزدان‌پرست is a compound adjective/noun 'God-worshipper' (i.e. pious or worshipper of God) used as a respectful epithet. The vocative 'کای شاه یزدان‌پرست' is direct speech opening.
segments.s766-g87: به بدگوهران 'to/against the ill‑natured' (بدگوهران = 'bad‑hearted' or wicked people) is the prepositional object. بر بس here is an intensifying phrase (بس = 'enough'; بر بس ≈ 'exceedingly/overly'), and ایمن مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become secure/safe' (مشو = 'do not become'). Together the phrase warns 'do not be too secure against bad people.'
segments.s766-g88: که here introduces the explanatory clause 'for/that': این را یکی داستانست نو = 'this is a single/new tale.' این را (this — object-marked) + یکی (one/a certain) + داستانست (is a tale; داستان + - است) + نو (new). The construction simply states that what was just related is only a tale/story.
segments.s766-g73: ورا is the older concatenated form of 'و' + 'را' meaning 'to him/it' (object/topic marker). برکشیدم is 1st‑person past ('I lifted/raised'). که here introduces a causal/explicative clause ('for/since'), and گوینده بود is a predicate clause 'was a speaker' with بود as the past copula.
segments.s766-g74: همان is the demonstrative 'that/same' modifying رزم (battle). را is the definite/object marker attaching to رزم, so رزم را = 'that battle' as the thing concerned. نیز 'also/too' adds inclusion, جوینده بود is a stative past clause 'was a seeker' — literally 'he was also a seeker of that same battle.' Word order (demonstrative + noun + را) is typical poetic placement.
segments.s766-g75: چو here functions as a temporal 'when' (in classical Persian often 'when' or 'as'). بی‌ارز را is fronted with the object marker را ('the one called "Bi-arez"/"worthless"'), and نام دادیم is the 1st‑person plural past 'we gave a name' (i.e. 'we named'). و then links to the following noun(s).
segments.s766-g76: This segment is a poetic list of nouns joined by repeated و ('and'): کنارنگی, پیل, مردان, مرز. In context they continue the naming/listing begun in the previous segment; repeated و is a rhythmic coordination. Each noun is simply coordinated (no extra inflection).
segments.s766-g77: اگر چند here forms a concessive phrase 'although/though' (classical usage: اگر + چند = 'though, even if'). بی‌مایه و بی‌تنست are two coordinated adjectives 'without substance/worth' and 'without body' describing the referent.
segments.s766-g78: برآورده precedes بارگاه and functions adjectivally ('fitting/suitable' or 'provided/fulfilled for'), with بارگاه 'court/palace' as the noun. منست is an older/stately predicate meaning 'is appropriate/it is fitting' (stative copula), so the clause means 'it is fitting for my court.' Word order (adjective + noun + predicate) is typical in classical verse.
segments.s766-g79: ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from', موبد is the agent 'mobed' (Zoroastrian priest), and شنیدستم is the archaic 1st‑person past 'I heard / I was told'. این داستان is the direct object 'this tale' (the object follows the verb here for poetic word order/emphasis).
segments.s766-g80: که introduces a relative/clausal complement 'who/which', برخواند is used in the sense 'recited/read aloud' (classical form of خواندن with prefix بر-), and از گفته باستان is a prepositional phrase 'from ancient sayings/accounts' specifying the source of the recitation.
segments.s766-g25: This clause gives time and action: دگر روز (literally 'another day' = 'the next day') precedes the locative برگاه ('royal court/platform') and the past tense verb بنشست ('sat'). شاه is the subject placed after the verb for literary word-order emphasis; overall it reads 'On the next day the king sat on the court.'
segments.s766-g26: به سر is a prepositional phrase 'on (the) head'; برنهاد is an archaic/polite past-tense verb 'placed/raised onto' (3rd singular). آن کیانی کلاه is the object 'that Kayanian hat/crown' following the verb. Together: 'he placed that Kayanian crown on his head.'
segments.s766-g27: یکی انجمن کرد is a light, slightly literary expression: یکی ('one' / 'a certain') + انجمن ('assembly') + کرد (past 'made/did') functions as 'he convened/held an assembly.' با introduces accompaniment ('with') and بخردان ('the wise') names those present.
segments.s766-g28: This is a coordinated noun phrase in apposition to 'بخردان': بزرگان ('the great ones/elders') and بیداردل موبدان ('the wakeful‑hearted mobeds'), where بیداردل is an adjectival compound preceding the noun موبدان. The whole string lists those who attended the council.
segments.s766-g29: This is reported speech: چه ('what') + بینید is the present/imperative form addressed to the assembly ('do you see' / 'what do you see'), and گفت is the reporting verb 'he said'; اندرین داستان ('in this tale') completes the quoted question. In effect: he said, 'What do you see in this story?'
segments.s766-g30: A second question in the same speech: چه ('what') + دارید ('do you have' — present 2nd/3rd person plural meaning 'have') + یاد ('memory, remembrance') and the prepositional phrase از گه باستان ('of/from the time of ancient days'). The construction یاد از <X> means 'memory/remembrance of X.'
segments.s766-g31: فرخ‌زاد گوید که uses گوید in the narrative (historic/presentive) 'says' with the complementizer که introducing reported speech or an indirect command. با انجمن ('with the assembly') is the accompanying prepositional phrase attached to that reported instruction.
segments.s766-g32: گذر کن is an imperative formed with the noun/verb stem گذر plus the auxiliary imperative کن = 'pass/go!' سوی is the preposition 'toward,' and بیشه نارون is the destination noun phrase 'the Nārūn grove.' The whole phrase commands movement toward that grove.
segments.s766-g105: خروشی (a substantive from خروش) is a verbal-noun meaning 'a cry/roar' placed as subject; برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth'. ز + لشکر marks the source ('from the army/host'). به زار is a circumstantial phrase 'in lamentation' (به = into/toward, زار = lament/wail), giving the manner of the cry.
segments.s766-g106: ز here gives the reason/cause ('because of' / 'from'). تیمار = 'care/concern' (here: anxiety/worry). وز in this position functions as a connector (classical poetic orthoepy 'و ز' = 'and/of'), but none of the provided senses match that use, so its meaning is noted as connective. رفتن شهریار is a noun phrase 'the king's going/departure' (رفتن as verbal-noun + شهریار).
segments.s766-g107: از ایشان 'from/of them' sets the domain. هر آنکس که is a relative/indefinite head 'whosoever/anyone who'. دهقان بدند uses بدند (classical past/plural of بودن) to state identity 'was a dehqan' (dehqan here = landed gentry). The clause thus identifies members among them.
segments.s766-g108: وگر = 'and if' / 'but if' introducing an alternative condition. خوی + ش = خویش ('his/one's own kin') formed by noun + possessive suffix ش. و links to پیوند 'bond/relation' followed by خاقان 'the khagan' (title). بدند again states 'were' (classical plural past), so the clause reads 'or if he was his own kin, or a relation of the khagan.'
segments.s766-g109: خروشان is a plural/collective participial adjective ('the roaring/clamorous ones') used as subject. بر + شهریار indicates direction/target ('upon/toward the king'). آمدند is past plural 'they came/approached.' The word order places the tumult before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s766-g110: همه دیده 'all eyes' is a nominative phrase (همه 'all' modifying دیده 'eyes'). چون juybar 'like a stream' is a simile modifying آمدند; here آمدند describes a flowing motion (eyes 'came/flowed'—i.e., teared up and streamed).
segments.s766-g111: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). ما را دل is a topical/object-marked construction (را marks دل as the theme: 'our heart' / 'the heart for us'). از بوم و آرامگاه uses از to indicate separation ('away from homeland and from resting-place/tomb'). Word order places the locative phrase after دل for emphasis.
segments.s766-g112: چگونه 'how' poses the rhetorical question; بود is past 'was' (here functioning as the copula/modal 'could be/was'), and شاد = 'happy/joyful.' بی + روی = 'without the face' (بی = without, روی = face/presence), and شاه specifies whose face: 'the king.' The sequence expresses the impossible happiness of hearts without the king present.
segments.s766-g41: A coordinated nominal phrase: 'بزرگان ایران' = 'the great men of Iran' (noun + genitive modifier), followed by 'و چندین سپاه' = 'and several armies/hosts.' There is no verb here; it's an asyndetic list typical in epic catalogue-lines.
segments.s766-g42: A continuation of the list: 'بر و بوم آباد' is a fixed collocation meaning 'land and home (prosperous/inhabited)', then 'و تخت و کلاه' = 'and the throne and the crown/hat.' The nouns are coordinated and the adjective 'آباد' qualifies 'بوم'.
segments.s766-g43: 'سر خویش' = 'my own head' (possessive formed by خوی + enclitic ش). The pair of verbs 'گیرم' and 'بمانم' are first-person forms used in a conditional/protasis ('if I take/keep... and remain...'), with 'بجای' 'in place' specifying where/position.
segments.s766-g44: Negative result clause: 'بزرگی نباشد' = 'there will be no greatness' (نباشد here expresses non‑existence/negation), followed by 'نه مردی و رای' = 'nor manliness and counsel' — a coordinated negative list of what will be lost.
segments.s766-g45: 'مرا' is expressed in the clause as two pieces here (tokenized as 'م' + 'را') marking the experiencer/affected party 'me' (object/dative). 'جنگ دشمن' is the subject 'the enemy's war', 'به آید' = 'comes upon' (verb phrase), and 'ز ننگ' 'from shame' gives the cause or origin. The whole is a protasis ('should ... happen to me').
segments.s766-g46: 'یکی داستان' = 'a certain tale/incident'; the past verb 'زد' ('struck') is used idiomatically to mean 'befell' or 'came to pass.' 'برین' = 'بر این' ('upon this/that') and 'بر پلنگ' = 'upon the panther/leopard' (a poetic epithet). Together it means 'a certain tale befell that leopard.'
segments.s766-g47: This is an admonitory subordinate clause introduced by 'که' ('that/so that'): 'خیره به' = 'staring' (adverbial quality), 'بدخواه' = 'ill‑wisher' with the preposition 'به' marking the target, and 'منمای' is a negative imperative 'do not show' (verb placed toward the end) with 'پشت' = 'back' as its object: 'do not show your back to an ill‑wisher.'
segments.s766-g48: 'چو' = 'when'; 'پیش آیدت' = 'comes before/befalls you' (آید + enclitic ت 'to you'); 'روزگاری درشت' = 'a harsh/rough time' where 'درشت' qualifies 'روزگاری.' The clause warns that in such a severe time the consequence will follow.
segments.s766-g65: بران is an adverbial/target marker here meaning ‘toward them’ (a contracted older form of بر آن). دوستی (“friendship”) is the object/theme placed before the verb; بیشی (‘more, greater’) is an adjectival/quantifying modifier of دوستی. کنیم is first-person plural subjunctive/hortative (‘let us do/make’), so the whole clause expresses a hortatory intention: ‘let us increase (our) friendship toward them.’
segments.s766-g66: که introduces a purposive/subordinate clause here (‘so that / in order that’). با + دخت فغفور marks the person with whom the kinship is to be formed (‘with the faghfur’s daughter’); دخت is the older form for ‘daughter’. خویشی کردن is a light-verb construction where خویشی (‘kinship/affinity’) is the noun and کنیم supplies the verbal/actional force ‘to make/become kin’. The verb is again subjunctive/hortative expressing purpose.
segments.s766-g67: بیاری here is an adverbial/agentive phrase meaning ‘by/with aid/companions’ (from یار), placed before the verb for emphasis. بیاید is a present/subjunctive third‑person form meaning ‘may come’ or ‘comes’ in a wish/optative sense. سپاهی گران is a nominal phrase where گران (‘heavy, mighty’) postmodifies سپاهی (‘army/host’) giving the sense ‘mighty/heavy host’. Word order (adverbial – verb – nominal) is poetic and topicalizing.
segments.s766-g68: This is a coordinated nominal list identifying who comprises the host: بزرگان ‘the great ones/nobles’ and ترکان جنگاوران ‘the Turkish warriors’. جنگاوران postmodifies ترکان (both plural) and the conjunction و links the two members of the list. The whole phrase apposes or completes سپاهی گران in the previous segment.
segments.s766-g69: کنارنگ + مروست is a locative phrase: کنارنگ (‘beside, at the edge’) and مروست (a place-name reference to Marv/Marvast) together mark origin/location (‘at the borders of Marv’). ماهوی نیز adds another locality or qualifier with نیز (‘also’). In this poetic ordering the place phrases precede the verb-clause that follows.
segments.s766-g70: ابا here functions as the main verb of the clause governing the list of direct objects that follow (لشکر و پیل و هر گونه چیز). In classical/poetic usage such short verbs can stand for ‘there came’ / ‘brought’ (contextually), and it governs a coordinate object: لشکر (‘army/host’) و پیل (‘and elephants’) و هر گونه چیز (‘and every kind of thing’). هر گونه is a frozen compound ‘every kind of’ modifying چیز. Note the coordinating و between items and the noun phrase هر گونه چیز capturing ‘all sorts of things.’
segments.s766-g71: کجا is the interrogative adverb ‘where’ placed at the clause‑initial position. پیشکار (‘attendant/steward’) is the predicate noun; شبانان ما is a genitival phrase ‘our shepherds’ (شبانان ‘shepherds’ + possessive ما after the head), and ست is the copula ‘is’. The construction is a direct question: ‘Where is the steward of our shepherds?’
segments.s766-g72: برآورده is used adjectivally/predicatively (a past participle or verbal adjective) meaning ‘brought forth / produced / provided’. It precedes the nominal phrase دشتبانان ما (our watchmen/herdsmen of the plain) and the clause closes with the copula ست, producing an emphatic identificational sentence: ‘It is our plain‑watchers who have brought (him) forth’ or ‘He has been brought forth by our watchmen.’ دشتبانان is the agent in this passive/agentive sense.
segments.s766-g129: مگر here introduces a hopeful/optative statement ('perhaps' / 'would that'); باز = 'again' modifies the seeing; بینم is 1st-person singular present-subjunctive/hortative of دیدن used as 'may I see' or 'let me see'; شما را is the person seen with the object marker را (marking 'one of you' as the object); یکی = 'one' (one person). Word order is typical older Persian: modal/introductory word + temporal adverb + verb + object (with را) + quantified object.
segments.s766-g130: شود is the 3rd-person present subjunctive/optative of شدن used here as 'may become' / 'happen that'; تیزی is used as 'swiftness/speed' (not just sharpness); تازیان is 'Taziān' in the sense of Arabian horses/Arabs' horses (the usual epic collocation with تیزی); اندکی is an adverb meaning 'a little' or 'to some small degree'. The verb comes before its complements (typical older word order).
segments.s766-g131: همه = 'all' or 'everyone'; پاک = 'pure' (adjective describing the group); پروردگار is 'the Lord' (here in a predicate phrase meaning 'of the Lord' or 'belonging to the Lord'); منید is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person-plural copula 'you are' (written here in two pieces in the word list). The line therefore predicates 'you are all pure [of] the Lord.'
segments.s766-g132: همان = 'the same' / 'likewise' introducing a parallel statement; از = 'from' marking origin; پدر = 'father' (source of the remembrance); یادگار = 'memento' / 'remembrance'; منید again is the archaic 2pl copula 'you are'. The phrase means 'you are likewise a memorial from (your) father.'
segments.s766-g133: نخواهم is 1st-person singular negative of خواستن: 'I do not want/I wish not'; که introduces the subordinate clause ('that'); آید is 3rd-person present-subjunctive of آمدن used in clauses of wishing/ fearing: 'that ... should come'; شما را is the affected party marked by را; گزند = 'harm'. The construction means 'I do not wish that harm come to you.'
segments.s766-g134: مباشید is the negative imperative/optative 2nd-person-plural form 'do not be' (poetic form of مباش'); با من = 'with me'; ببد here is a poetic/metrical form representing بد 'bad' (an archaism or expressive doubling for meter); یارمند = 'ally, helper' (a compound meaning 'one who is like a friend/ally'). The clause is an appeal: 'Do not be, with me, a bad ally' → 'do not be an ill ally to me.'
segments.s766-g135: ببینیم is 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/hortative of دیدن used as 'let us see' or 'we shall see'; تا here functions temporally/purposively 'until' / 'to see whether'; گرد گردان سپهر is a compound image: گرد (round/circle) + گردان (revolving) + سپهر (the sky/heaven) — 'the revolving circle/orb of the sky' (a poetic way to speak of the heavens' rotation).
segments.s766-g136: ازین = از + این 'from this' (از marks origin or direction); سو = 'side' / 'toward' so ازین سو = 'from this side' or 'to which side' in context; کنون = 'now'; بر = preposition 'toward/on' here 'toward'; که introduces the subordinate content 'whom/which'; گردد is 3rd-person present-subjunctive of گشتن/گردیدن 'turn' (here 'will turn'); به مهر = 'with favour/kindness' (مهر = favour/kindness). Together: 'to which side the revolving heavens will now turn with favour.'
